name: Romance Languages
description: Distinguish between French, Spanish, Portuguese, and Italian.

questions.text: Onde fica a biblioteca?
questions.explanation: The word 'Onde' (where) and the use of 'fica' for location are characteristic of Portuguese.

questions.text: Dove si trova la biblioteca?
questions.explanation: Italian uses 'Dove' for where and 'si trova' (finds itself) for location.

questions.text: ¿Dónde está la biblioteca?
questions.explanation: Spanish uses the inverted question mark and 'dónde' with an accent.

questions.text: Où se trouve la bibliothèque ?
questions.explanation: The spelling 'bibliothèque' and the grave accent on 'Où' are unique to French here.

questions.text: Voudriez-vous un peu de pain ?
questions.explanation: The use of 'vous' and the word 'pain' (bread) are distinctly French.

questions.text: Quisiera un poco de pan, por favor.
questions.explanation: 'Quisiera' is a common Spanish polite request, and 'pan' is the Spanish word for bread.

questions.text: Eu gostaria de um pouco de pão.
questions.explanation: The nasal 'ão' in 'pão' is a major phonetic marker for Portuguese.

questions.text: Vorrei un po' di pane, per favore.
questions.explanation: The conditional 'Vorrei' and the ending in 'pane' identify this as Italian.

questions.text: Il fait très froid aujourd'hui.
questions.explanation: 'Aujourd'hui' is one of the most uniquely recognizable words in the French language.

questions.text: Hace mucho frío hoy.
questions.explanation: The use of 'Hace' for weather and 'hoy' for today is standard Spanish.

questions.text: Está muito frio hoje.
questions.explanation: Portuguese uses 'hoje' for today, which is similar but spelled differently than Spanish 'hoy'.

questions.text: Fa molto freddo oggi.
questions.explanation: Italian uses 'oggi' for today and 'freddo' with a double 'd'.

questions.text: Mangio una mela rossa.
questions.explanation: The word 'mela' (apple) and 'mangio' (I eat) are Italian.

questions.text: Como una manzana roja.
questions.explanation: 'Manzana' and 'roja' are classic Spanish vocabulary words.

questions.text: Eu como uma maçã vermelha.
questions.explanation: The word 'maçã' with the cedilla and tilde is uniquely Portuguese.

questions.text: Je mange une pomme rouge.
questions.explanation: 'Je mange' and 'pomme' (apple) are French.

questions.text: Qu'est-ce que tu fais ce soir ?
questions.explanation: The 'Qu'est-ce que' construction is a staple of French questioning.

questions.text: ¿Qué haces esta noche?
questions.explanation: Spanish uses 'noche' for night/evening and 'haces' for 'you do'.

questions.text: O que você vai fazer hoje à noite?
questions.explanation: 'Você' is the common second-person pronoun in Brazilian Portuguese.

questions.text: Cosa fai stasera?
questions.explanation: 'Stasera' is the Italian contraction for 'questa sera' (this evening).

questions.text: Arrivederci, a domani!
questions.explanation: 'Arrivederci' is a globally recognized Italian farewell.

questions.text: ¡Hasta mañana!
questions.explanation: The tilde on the 'ñ' in 'mañana' is a key Spanish identifier.

questions.text: Até amanhã!
questions.explanation: Portuguese uses 'nh' instead of 'ñ' for the same sound.

questions.text: À demain !
questions.explanation: French uses 'demain' for tomorrow and the 'À' preposition for 'until'.

questions.text: La fenêtre est ouverte.
questions.explanation: The circumflex on 'fenêtre' (window) is very common in French spelling.

questions.text: La ventana está abierta.
questions.explanation: 'Ventana' and 'abierta' are standard Spanish vocabulary.

questions.text: A janela está aberta.
questions.explanation: Portuguese uses 'janela' for window, distinct from the other three.

questions.text: La finestra è aperta.
questions.explanation: The 'è' (is) with a grave accent is an Italian marker.

questions.text: Mi piace molto viaggiare.
questions.explanation: 'Mi piace' is the Italian way to express liking something.

questions.text: Me gusta mucho viajar.
questions.explanation: 'Me gusta' is the Spanish equivalent of 'I like'.

questions.text: Eu gosto muito de viajar.
questions.explanation: Portuguese requires the preposition 'de' after 'gostar'.

questions.text: J'aime beaucoup voyager.
questions.explanation: The contraction 'J'aime' and the adverb 'beaucoup' are French.

questions.text: Il y a un chat dans le jardin.
questions.explanation: 'Il y a' means 'there is' in French; 'chat' is cat.

questions.text: Hay un gato en el jardín.
questions.explanation: 'Hay' is 'there is' in Spanish, and 'jardín' has the Spanish acute accent.

questions.text: Há um gato no jardim.
questions.explanation: Portuguese uses 'Há' (there is) and 'no' (contraction of in + the).

questions.text: C'è un gatto nel giardino.
questions.explanation: Italian uses 'C'è' for 'there is' and 'nel' for 'in the'.

questions.text: Parlez-vous anglais ?
questions.explanation: The hyphenated verb-pronoun inversion is typical of French questions.

questions.text: ¿Habla usted inglés?
questions.explanation: 'Usted' is the formal 'you' in Spanish; 'inglés' has the Spanish accent.

questions.text: Você fala inglês?
questions.explanation: In Portuguese, 'inglês' is spelled with a circumflex accent.

questions.text: Parla inglese?
questions.explanation: Italian often omits the subject pronoun ('Lei') in this question.

questions.text: Sempre sognando un futuro migliore.
questions.explanation: The double 'n' and ending in 'ore' are common Italian traits.

questions.text: Siempre soñando con un futuro mejor.
questions.explanation: The 'ie' in 'Siempre' and the word 'mejor' are Spanish.

questions.text: Sempre sonhando com um futuro melhor.
questions.explanation: The 'nh' in 'sonhando' is a Portuguese phonetic marker.

questions.text: Toujours rêvant d'un avenir meilleur.
questions.explanation: 'Toujours' and 'avenir' (future) are French.

questions.text: L'addition, s'il vous plaît.
questions.explanation: 'L'addition' is the specific French term for a restaurant bill.

questions.text: La cuenta, por favor.
questions.explanation: 'La cuenta' is the Spanish word for the bill.

questions.text: A conta, por favor.
questions.explanation: The Portuguese 'A' as a feminine article is a key marker.

questions.text: Il conto, per favore.
questions.explanation: Italian uses 'Il' as the masculine article and 'conto' for the bill.

questions.text: C'est une belle journée.
questions.explanation: 'C'est' and 'journée' (day) are French.

questions.text: È una bella giornata.
questions.explanation: 'Bella' and 'giornata' are Italian descriptors for a nice day.

questions.text: Es un día hermoso.
questions.explanation: 'Hermoso' (beautiful) and 'día' (day) are Spanish.

questions.text: É um dia bonito.
questions.explanation: Portuguese uses 'É' (is) with an acute accent and 'bonito' (beautiful).

questions.text: Quels sont vos projets ?
questions.explanation: The plural 'Quels' and 'projets' with a silent 's' are French.

questions.text: ¿Cuáles son sus planes?
questions.explanation: Spanish uses 'Cuáles' for 'which ones' and 'planes' for plans.

questions.text: Quais são os seus planos?
questions.explanation: 'Quais são' is the Portuguese plural for 'which are'.

questions.text: Quali sono i tuoi progetti?
questions.explanation: 'Quali' and 'tuoi' are Italian plural markers.

questions.text: Merci beaucoup pour votre aide.
questions.explanation: 'Merci' and 'aide' (help) are French.

questions.text: Muchas gracias por su ayuda.
questions.explanation: 'Muchas gracias' and 'ayuda' (help) are Spanish.

questions.text: Muito obrigado pela sua ajuda.
questions.explanation: 'Obrigado' and the contraction 'pela' (por + a) are Portuguese.

questions.text: Grazie mille per il tuo aiuto.
questions.explanation: 'Grazie mille' (thousand thanks) and 'aiuto' are Italian.

questions.text: Se eu tivesse mais tempo, eu viajaria pelo mundo.
questions.explanation: The imperfect subjunctive 'tivesse' and the conditional 'viajaria' are distinctively Portuguese.

questions.text: Si tuviera más tiempo, viajaría por el mundo.
questions.explanation: The Spanish imperfect subjunctive 'tuviera' and the use of 'por' for 'through' identify this.

questions.text: Se avessi più tempo, viaggerei per il mondo.
questions.explanation: The Italian double 's' in 'avessi' and the conditional ending 'ei' in 'viaggerei' are key.

questions.text: Si j'avais plus de temps, je voyagerais à travers le monde.
questions.explanation: The French 'avais' (imperfect) and the complex 'à travers le monde' are unique here.

questions.text: L'enfant joue avec un petit oiseau dans la forêt.
questions.explanation: The word 'oiseau' (bird) contains almost all French vowels and is a classic identifier.

questions.text: El niño juega con un pajarito en el bosque.
questions.explanation: 'Niño' (boy) and 'bosque' (forest) are standard Spanish vocabulary.

questions.text: O menino brinca com um passarinho na floresta.
questions.explanation: The word 'menino' and the 'fl' in 'floresta' are typical Portuguese markers.

questions.text: Il bambino gioca con un uccellino nel bosco.
questions.explanation: The double consonants in 'bambino' and 'uccellino' are very characteristic of Italian.

questions.text: È necessario che tu faccia i compiti ogni giorno.
questions.explanation: The subjunctive 'faccia' and the word 'compiti' (homework) are Italian.

questions.text: Es necesario que hagas la tarea todos los días.
questions.explanation: The subjunctive 'hagas' and 'tarea' (homework) identify this as Spanish.

questions.text: É necessário que você faça os deveres todos os dias.
questions.explanation: The tilde in 'necessário' and 'faça' with a cedilla are Portuguese markers.

questions.text: Il est nécessaire que tu fasses tes devoirs chaque jour.
questions.explanation: The spelling of 'nécessaire' and the subjunctive 'fasses' are French.

questions.text: Acredito que eles chegarão amanhã de manhã.
questions.explanation: The future ending 'ão' in 'chegarão' is a major Portuguese giveaway.

questions.text: Creo que llegarán mañana por la mañana.
questions.explanation: The future ending 'án' and the word 'mañana' are Spanish.

questions.text: Credo che arriveranno domani mattina.
questions.explanation: The double 'r' in 'arriveranno' and 'mattina' are classic Italian.

questions.text: Je crois qu'ils arriveront demain matin.
questions.explanation: The French 'crois' and 'arriveront' identify this sentence.

questions.text: Voglio mangiare una pizza deliziosa in quel ristorante.
questions.explanation: The 'gl' sound in 'voglio' and the ending of 'ristorante' are Italian.

questions.text: Quero comer uma pizza deliciosa naquele restaurante.
questions.explanation: The contraction 'naquele' (em + aquele) is a specific Portuguese feature.

questions.text: Quiero comer una pizza deliciosa en ese restaurante.
questions.explanation: The 'ie' in 'Quiero' and the demonstrative 'ese' are Spanish.

questions.text: Je veux manger une pizza délicieuse dans ce restaurant.
questions.explanation: The French 'veux' and the spelling 'délicieuse' are unique identifiers.

questions.text: O cachorro está latindo para o gato preto.
questions.explanation: Portuguese uses 'cachorro' for dog and the 'o' article before it.

questions.text: El perro está ladrando al gato negro.
questions.explanation: 'Perro' and the color 'negro' are standard Spanish.

questions.text: Le chien aboie après le chat noir.
questions.explanation: 'Chien' and 'noir' (black) are common French words.

questions.text: Il cane abbaia al gatto nero.
questions.explanation: The word 'cane' and 'gatto' are Italian markers here.

questions.text: Nous allons à la plage ce week-end.
questions.explanation: The French verb 'allons' and the noun 'plage' are distinct.

questions.text: Vamos a la playa este fin de semana.
questions.explanation: The word 'playa' (beach) and 'fin de semana' are Spanish.

questions.text: Andiamo in spiaggia questo fine settimana.
questions.explanation: The Italian 'andiamo' and 'spiaggia' (beach) with double 'g' are key.

questions.text: Nós vamos à praia este fim de semana.
questions.explanation: The word 'praia' and the grave accent on 'à' identify Portuguese.

questions.text: I miei genitori vivono in una grande città.
questions.explanation: The plural possessive 'I miei' and 'città' with the accent are Italian.

questions.text: Mis padres viven en una gran ciudad.
questions.explanation: Spanish uses 'padres' for parents and 'ciudad' for city.

questions.text: Meus pais vivem em uma cidade grande.
questions.explanation: 'Meus pais' and 'cidade' are common Portuguese markers.

questions.text: Mes parents vivent dans une grande ville.
questions.explanation: 'Mes parents' and 'ville' (city) are French terms.

questions.text: Elle lit un livre intéressant dans sa chambre.
questions.explanation: The pronoun 'Elle' and the word 'livre' are French.

questions.text: Ella lee un libro interesante en su habitación.
questions.explanation: The double 'e' in 'lee' (reads) and 'habitación' are Spanish.

questions.text: Lei legge un libro interessante nella sua camera.
questions.explanation: The double 'g' in 'legge' and the pronoun 'Lei' are Italian.

questions.text: Ela lê um livro interessante no seu quarto.
questions.explanation: The circumflex in 'lê' and the word 'quarto' (room) are Portuguese.

questions.text: Temos que comprar leite e ovos no mercado.
questions.explanation: The nasal 'em' and words like 'leite' and 'ovos' are Portuguese.

questions.text: Tenemos que comprar leche y huevos en el mercado.
questions.explanation: 'Leche' and 'huevos' (eggs) are standard Spanish.

questions.text: Dobbiamo comprare latte e uova al mercato.
questions.explanation: The double 'b' in 'dobbiamo' and 'latte' (milk) are Italian.

questions.text: Nous devons acheter du lait et des œufs au marché.
questions.explanation: The French partitive articles 'du' and 'des' identify this sentence.

questions.text: L'eau est très froide ce matin.
questions.explanation: The word 'eau' (water) is a classic French vowel-heavy word.

questions.text: L'acqua è molto fredda questa mattina.
questions.explanation: The spelling 'acqua' with 'cq' is unique to Italian.

questions.text: El agua está muy fría esta mañana.
questions.explanation: Spanish uses 'agua' and the feminine 'esta mañana'.

questions.text: A água está muito fria esta manhã.
questions.explanation: Portuguese uses 'A' as the article for 'água'.

questions.text: Per favore, chiuda la porta quando esce.
questions.explanation: The spelling 'chiuda' (close) and 'porta' are Italian.

questions.text: Por favor, cierre la puerta cuando salga.
questions.explanation: The Spanish 'ie' in 'cierre' and the subjunctive 'salga' are key.

questions.text: Por favor, feche a porta quando sair.
questions.explanation: The word 'feche' (close) is unique to Portuguese among these.

questions.text: S'il vous plaît, fermez la porte quand vous sortez.
questions.explanation: The verb 'fermez' and 'sortez' identify this as French.

questions.text: Cette voiture rouge est très rapide.
questions.explanation: The word 'voiture' (car) is a common French term.

questions.text: Este coche rojo es muy rápido.
questions.explanation: The word 'coche' and the color 'rojo' are Spanish identifiers.

questions.text: Este carro vermelho é muito rápido.
questions.explanation: Portuguese uses 'carro' and the color 'vermelho'.

questions.text: Questa macchina rossa è molto veloce.
questions.explanation: Italian uses 'macchina' for car and 'rossa' for the color red.

questions.text: Il pleut toujours en automne ici.
questions.explanation: 'Il pleut' (it rains) and 'automne' are French.

questions.text: Llueve siempre en otoño aquí.
questions.explanation: The double 'l' in 'Llueve' and 'otoño' with the 'ñ' are Spanish.

questions.text: Chove sempre no outono aqui.
questions.explanation: The 'ch' in 'Chove' and 'outono' are Portuguese identifiers.

questions.text: Piove sempre in autunno qui.
questions.explanation: The word 'piove' (it rains) and 'autunno' are Italian.

questions.text: Vorrei un bicchiere di vino rosso.
questions.explanation: The spelling 'bicchiere' (glass) is a classic Italian identifier.

questions.text: Quisiera una copa de vino tinto.
questions.explanation: Spanish uses 'copa' for wine glass and 'tinto' for red wine.

questions.text: Eu gostaria de um copo de vinho tinto.
questions.explanation: Portuguese uses 'copo' (which looks like Spanish 'copa' but is masculine).

questions.text: Je voudrais un verre de vin rouge.
questions.explanation: The French 'verre' (glass) and 'vin' are distinct here.

questions.text: Spero che tu possa venire alla festa domani sera, nonostante l'impegno.
questions.explanation: The use of the subjunctive 'possa' and the double consonants in 'festa' and 'notostante' are key Italian markers.

questions.text: Espero que possas vir à festa amanhã à noite, apesar do compromisso.
questions.explanation: The personal infinitive 'possas' and the grave accent in 'à' are characteristic of Portuguese.

questions.text: Espero que puedas venir a la fiesta mañana por la noche, a pesar del compromiso.
questions.explanation: The diphthong 'ue' in 'puedas' and the use of 'mañana por la noche' are distinctively Spanish.

questions.text: J'espère que tu pourras venir à la fête demain soir, malgré ton engagement.
questions.explanation: The circumflex accent in 'fête' and the future tense 'pourras' (rather than subjunctive) after 'espérer' are French traits.

questions.text: Si j'avais su qu'il pleuvrait, j'aurais apporté mon parapluie.
questions.explanation: The structure 'Si' + pluperfect followed by conditional 'aurais' is a classic French hypothetical.

questions.text: Se avessi saputo che sarebbe piovuto, avrei portato l'ombrello.
questions.explanation: The double 's' in 'avessi' and the word 'ombrello' (umbrella) are typical Italian features.

questions.text: Si hubiera sabido que iba a llover, habría traído mi paraguas.
questions.explanation: The past subjunctive 'hubiera' and the compound word 'paraguas' are uniquely Spanish.

questions.text: Se eu soubesse que ia chover, teria trazido o meu guarda-chuva.
questions.explanation: The spelling 'soubesse' and the hyphenated 'guarda-chuva' identify this as Portuguese.

questions.text: Il faut que nous fassions attention aux détails de ce projet important.
questions.explanation: The impersonal 'Il faut que' and the nasal 'nous fassions' are distinct French markers.

questions.text: Es necesario que prestemos atención a los detalles de este proyecto importante.
questions.explanation: The verb 'prestar' in this context and the 'oy' in 'proyecto' are common in Spanish.

questions.text: É necessário que prestemos atenção aos detalhes deste projeto importante.
questions.explanation: The opening 'É' with an acute accent and the contraction 'deste' are Portuguese identifiers.

questions.text: È necessario che prestiamo attenzione ai dettagli di questo progetto importante.
questions.explanation: The grave accent on 'È' and the plural 'dettagli' ending in 'i' indicate Italian.

questions.text: Ho cercato di chiamarti più volte, ma il tuo telefono era sempre spento.
questions.explanation: The auxiliary 'Ho' and the word 'spento' (off/extinguished) are specific to Italian.

questions.text: He intentado llamarte varias veces, pero tu teléfono estaba siempre apagado.
questions.explanation: The present perfect 'He intentado' and the word 'apagado' (off) are typical Spanish.

questions.text: Tentei ligar-te várias vezes, mas o teu telemóvel estava sempre desligado.
questions.explanation: The enclitic pronoun 'ligar-te' and the term 'telemóvel' (common in European Portuguese) mark this language.

questions.text: J'ai essayé de t'appeler plusieurs fois, mais ton téléphone était toujours éteint.
questions.explanation: The apostrophe in 't'appeler' and the word 'éteint' are French characteristics.

questions.text: Ninguém sabe ao certo onde eles esconderam o tesouro antigo.
questions.explanation: The word 'Ninguém' (nobody) and the nasal 'em' ending are hallmarks of Portuguese.

questions.text: Nadie sabe con certeza dónde escondieron el tesoro antiguo.
questions.explanation: The word 'Nadie' and the accent on 'dónde' distinguish this as Spanish.

questions.text: Nessuno sa con certezza dove abbiano nascosto il tesoro antico.
questions.explanation: The word 'Nessuno' and the past participle 'nascosto' are Italian markers.

questions.text: Personne ne sait avec certitude où ils ont caché le trésor antique.
questions.explanation: The double negation 'Personne ne' and the 'ch' in 'caché' are distinctively French.

questions.text: Bien que nous soyons fatigués, nous continuerons à marcher jusqu'au village.
questions.explanation: The conjunction 'Bien que' followed by the subjunctive 'soyons' is a French indicator.

questions.text: Aunque estemos cansados, seguiremos caminando hasta el pueblo.
questions.explanation: The word 'Aunque' and the 'll' in 'pueblo' (meaning village) are Spanish.

questions.text: Embora estejamos cansados, continuaremos a caminhar até à aldeia.
questions.explanation: The word 'Embora' and the nasal 'caminhar' are Portuguese identifiers.

questions.text: Sebbene siamo stanchi, continueremo a camminare fino al villaggio.
questions.explanation: The word 'Sebbene' and the double 'm' in 'camminare' indicate Italian.

questions.text: Qualcuno ha lasciato una borsa di pelle sul sedile posteriore dell'auto.
questions.explanation: The word 'Qualcuno' (someone) and 'borsa' (bag) are Italian terms.

questions.text: Alguém deixou uma bolsa de couro no banco de trás do carro.
questions.explanation: The nasal 'Alguém' and the word 'couro' (leather) are Portuguese.

questions.text: Alguien dejó un bolso de cuero en el asiento trasero del coche.
questions.explanation: The word 'Alguien' and 'asiento' (seat) identify this as Spanish.

questions.text: Quelqu'un a laissé un sac en cuir sur le siège arrière de la voiture.
questions.explanation: The word 'Quelqu'un' and the spelling 'siège' are classic French.

questions.text: Il me semble que vous avez oublié vos clés sur la table du salon.
questions.explanation: The phrase 'Il me semble' and the plural 'clés' (keys) are French.

questions.text: Mi sembra che abbiate dimenticato le chiavi sul tavolo del soggiorno.
questions.explanation: The verb 'dimenticato' and the 'chi' in 'chiavi' are Italian.

questions.text: Parece-me que esqueceram as chaves em cima da mesa da sala.
questions.explanation: The hyphenated 'Parece-me' and the 'ch' in 'chaves' mark this as Portuguese.

questions.text: Me parece que habéis olvidado las llaves sobre la mesa del salón.
questions.explanation: The 'll' in 'llaves' and the 'éis' ending in 'habéis' (Spain Spanish) are Spanish markers.

questions.text: Le petit garçon courait dans le jardin avec son chien fidèle.
questions.explanation: The articles 'le' and the word 'chien' (dog) are very French.

questions.text: El niño pequeño corría por el jardín con su perro fiel.
questions.explanation: The word 'niño' with the tilde (ñ) and 'perro' (dog) are Spanish.

questions.text: O menino pequeno corria pelo jardim com o seu cão fiel.
questions.explanation: The word 'menino' and the 'ão' in 'cão' (dog) are Portuguese markers.

questions.text: Il bambino piccolo correva nel giardino con il suo cane fedele.
questions.explanation: The word 'bambino' and 'cane' (dog) are Italian identifiers.

questions.text: Si vous voulez réussir, vous devez travailler avec beaucoup de persévérance.
questions.explanation: The spelling of 'réussir' and 'beaucoup' are characteristic of French.

questions.text: Se volete avere successo, dovete lavorare con molta perseveranza.
questions.explanation: The word 'successo' and the adverb 'molta' are Italian features.

questions.text: Se quiserem ter sucesso, devem trabalhar com muita perseverança.
questions.explanation: The future subjunctive 'quiserem' and 'muita' are Portuguese markers.

questions.text: Si queréis tener éxito, debéis trabajar con mucha perseverancia.
questions.explanation: The word 'éxito' and the 'éis' endings are characteristic of Spanish.

questions.text: L'insegnante ha spiegato la lezione in modo molto chiaro oggi.
questions.explanation: The word 'insegnante' and 'chiaro' with 'chi' are typical of Italian.

questions.text: O professor explicou a lição de forma muito clara hoje.
questions.explanation: The nasal 'lição' and the word 'hoje' (today) are Portuguese markers.

questions.text: El profesor explicó la lección de forma muy clara hoy.
questions.explanation: The word 'lección' and 'hoy' (today) distinguish this as Spanish.

questions.text: Le professeur a expliqué la leçon de manière très claire aujourd'hui.
questions.explanation: The word 'aujourd'hui' and the 'très' are uniquely French.

questions.text: Nous irons à la plage dès que le temps se réchauffera un peu.
questions.explanation: The word 'plage' and the future 'réchauffera' are French.

questions.text: Andremo in spiaggia non appena il tempo si scalderà un po'.
questions.explanation: The word 'spiaggia' and 'non appena' (as soon as) are Italian.

questions.text: Iremos a la playa tan pronto como el tiempo se caliente un poco.
questions.explanation: The word 'playa' and 'tan pronto como' identify this as Spanish.

questions.text: Iremos à praia assim que o tempo aquecer um pouco.
questions.explanation: The word 'praia' and the future subjunctive 'aquecer' are Portuguese.

questions.text: Todos os dias ela costuma caminhar pela beira-mar ao pôr do sol.
questions.explanation: The circumflex in 'pôr' and the word 'beira-mar' are Portuguese markers.

questions.text: Todos los días ella suele caminar por la orilla del mar al atardecer.
questions.explanation: The verb 'suele' and the word 'atardecer' are Spanish features.

questions.text: Ogni giorno lei è solita camminare lungo il mare al tramonto.
questions.explanation: The phrase 'Ogni giorno' and the word 'tramonto' are Italian.

questions.text: Chaque jour, elle a l'habitude de marcher au bord de la mer au coucher du soleil.
questions.explanation: The phrase 'Chaque jour' and 'coucher du soleil' (sunset) are French.

questions.text: Je ne pense pas qu'il soit nécessaire d'acheter un nouveau livre maintenant.
questions.explanation: The negation 'ne... pas' and the subjunctive 'soit' are French markers.

questions.text: Non credo che sia necessario comprare un nuovo libro adesso.
questions.explanation: The word 'adesso' and the subjunctive 'sia' are Italian features.

questions.text: Não creio que seja necessário comprar um livro novo agora.
questions.explanation: The negation 'Não' and the word 'agora' are Portuguese markers.

questions.text: No creo que sea necesario comprar un libro nuevo ahora.
questions.explanation: The negation 'No' and the word 'ahora' distinguish this as Spanish.

questions.text: S'il vous plaît, pourriez-vous me dire où se trouve la gare la plus proche ?
questions.explanation: The phrase 'S'il vous plaît' and the word 'gare' (train station) are French.

questions.text: Per favore, potrebbe dirmi dove si trova la stazione ferroviaria più vicina?
questions.explanation: The phrase 'Per favore' and 'più vicina' identify this as Italian.

questions.text: Por favor, ¿podría decirme dónde está la estación de tren más cercana?
questions.explanation: The phrase 'Por favor' and the word 'cercana' are Spanish features.

questions.text: Por favor, poderia dizer-me onde fica a estação de comboios mais próxima?
questions.explanation: The word 'comboios' (trains) and 'próxima' are Portuguese markers.

questions.text: O vento soprava com muita força enquanto atravessávamos a ponte de ferro.
questions.explanation: The 'ão' in 'enquanto' and the word 'ponte' are Portuguese.

questions.text: Il vento soffiava con molta forza mentre attraversavamo il ponte di ferro.
questions.explanation: The word 'mentre' and the verb 'soffiava' are Italian features.

questions.text: El viento soplaba con mucha fuerza mientras cruzábamos el puente de hierro.
questions.explanation: The word 'mientras' and 'hierro' (iron) are Spanish markers.

questions.text: Le vent soufflait très fort pendant que nous traversions le pont de fer.
questions.explanation: The word 'pendant' and the 'tr' in 'traversions' are French.

questions.text: Guarda come corrono velocemente quei bambini nel prato fiorito.
questions.explanation: The word 'Guarda' and 'fiorito' identify this as Italian.

questions.text: Regarde comme ces enfants courent vite dans la prairie fleurie.
questions.explanation: The word 'Regarde' and the spelling 'prairie' are French markers.

questions.text: Mira cómo corren rápido esos niños en el prado florido.
questions.explanation: The word 'Mira' and the 'ñ' in 'niños' are Spanish identifiers.

questions.text: Olha como essas crianças correm depressa no prado florido.
questions.explanation: The word 'Olha' and 'crianças' (children) are Portuguese markers.

questions.text: Estou muito cansado porque tive um dia de trabalho muito longo.
questions.explanation: The word 'Estou' and the nasal 'um' are Portuguese markers.

questions.text: Estoy muy cansado porque he tenido un día de trabajo muy largo.
questions.explanation: The word 'Estoy' and 'largo' distinguish this as Spanish.

questions.text: Sono molto stanco perché ho avuto una giornata di lavoro molto lunga.
questions.explanation: The word 'Sono' and 'stanco' identify this as Italian.

questions.text: Je suis très fatigué parce que j'ai eu une très longue journée de travail.
questions.explanation: The phrase 'Je suis' and the spelling 'journée' are French.

questions.text: Elle s'est assise sur le banc pour lire son roman préféré tranquillement.
questions.explanation: The word 'banc' and the adverb 'tranquillement' are French features.

questions.text: Si è seduta sulla panchina per leggere il suo romanzo preferito in pace.
questions.explanation: The word 'panchina' (bench) and 'romanzo' (novel) are Italian.

questions.text: Ella se sentó en el banco para leer su novela favorita tranquilamente.
questions.explanation: The word 'novela' and the 'll' in 'ella' are Spanish markers.

questions.text: Ela sentou-se no banco para ler o seu romance preferido tranquilamente.
questions.explanation: The reflexive 'sentou-se' and the word 'romance' identify this as Portuguese.

questions.text: Questi stivali sono troppo stretti per me, devo cambiarli domani.
questions.explanation: The word 'stivali' (boots) and 'stretti' (tight) are Italian.

questions.text: Estas botas são muito apertadas para mim, tenho de as trocar amanhã.
questions.explanation: The word 'botas' and 'amanhã' are Portuguese identifiers.

questions.text: Estas botas son demasiado estrechas para mí, tengo que cambiarlas mañana.
questions.explanation: The word 'mañana' and 'estrechas' (narrow/tight) are Spanish markers.

questions.text: Ces bottes sont trop étroites pour moi, je dois les changer demain.
questions.explanation: The word 'bottes' and the circumflex in 'étroites' are French.

questions.text: Il pleut des cordes depuis ce matin, donc je resterai à la maison.
questions.explanation: The idiom 'pleut des cordes' (raining cats and dogs) and 'maison' are French.

questions.text: Está a chover torrencialmente desde manhã, por isso ficarei em casa.
questions.explanation: The construction 'está a chover' and 'casa' are Portuguese markers.

questions.text: Está lloviendo a cántaros desde esta mañana, así que me quedaré en casa.
questions.explanation: The idiom 'lloviendo a cántaros' and 'quedaré' are Spanish identifiers.

questions.text: Piove a dirotto da stamattina, quindi rimarrò a casa.
questions.explanation: The idiom 'piove a dirotto' and 'quindi' (therefore) are Italian.

questions.text: Chiunque abbia trovato le mie chiavi è pregato di restituirle subito.
questions.explanation: The word 'Chiunque' (whoever) and 'subito' (immediately) are Italian.

questions.text: Quem quer que tenha encontrado as minhas chaves, por favor, devolva-as agora.
questions.explanation: The phrase 'Quem quer que' and 'encontrado' are Portuguese.

questions.text: Quienquiera que haya encontrado mis llaves, por favor, devuélvalas ahora.
questions.explanation: The word 'Quienquiera' and 'haya' are Spanish features.

questions.text: Quiconque a trouvé mes clés est prié de les rendre immédiatement.
questions.explanation: The word 'Quiconque' and 'immédiatement' are French identifiers.

questions.text: Nous devrions prendre le petit-déjeuner avant de partir pour la montagne.
questions.explanation: The word 'petit-déjeuner' and 'montagne' are French markers.

questions.text: Dovremmo fare colazione prima di partire per la montagna.
questions.explanation: The word 'colazione' (breakfast) and 'partire' are Italian markers.

questions.text: Deberíamos desayunar antes de salir para la montaña.
questions.explanation: The verb 'desayunar' and 'salir' identify this as Spanish.

questions.text: Deveríamos tomar o pequeno-almoço antes de partirmos para a montanha.
questions.explanation: The term 'pequeno-almoço' (common in Portugal) and 'partirmos' are Portuguese.

questions.text: O gato está dormindo tranquilamente em cima do tapete da sala.
questions.explanation: The gerund 'dormindo' and 'tapete' (rug) are Portuguese markers.

questions.text: Il gatto sta dormendo tranquillamente sopra il tappeto del soggiorno.
questions.explanation: The double 'p' in 'tappeto' and 'soggiorno' (living room) are Italian.

questions.text: El gato está durmiendo tranquilamente encima de la alfombra del salón.
questions.explanation: The word 'alfombra' (rug) and 'durmiendo' identify this as Spanish.

questions.text: Le chat dort tranquillement sur le tapis du salon.
questions.explanation: The word 'chat' (cat) and 'tapis' are French identifiers.

questions.text: L'eau du lac est très froide à cette période de l'année.
questions.explanation: The word 'l'eau' (water) and 'lac' (lake) are French.

questions.text: L'acqua del lago è molto fredda in questo periodo dell'anno.
questions.explanation: The word 'l'acqua' and 'lago' identify this as Italian.

questions.text: El agua del lago está muy fría en esta época del año.
questions.explanation: The word 'agua' and 'época' are Spanish markers.

questions.text: A água do lago está muito fria nesta época do ano.
questions.explanation: The word 'água' and the contraction 'nesta' are Portuguese.

questions.text: Spero che voi abbiate passato una bella vacanza al mare.
questions.explanation: The word 'abbiate' and 'vacanza' are Italian identifiers.

questions.text: Espero que vocês tenham passado umas ótimas férias na praia.
questions.explanation: The word 'vocês' and 'tenham' are Portuguese markers.

questions.text: Espero que hayáis pasado unas buenas vacaciones en la playa.
questions.explanation: The word 'hayáis' and 'playa' identify this as Spanish.

questions.text: J'espère que vous avez passé de bonnes vacances à la mer.
questions.explanation: The word 'passé' and 'vacances' are French markers.

questions.text: Le ciel est bleu aujourd'hui et il n'y a pas de nuages.
questions.explanation: The word 'bleu' and 'nuages' (clouds) are French.

questions.text: Il cielo è azzurro oggi e non ci sono nuvole.
questions.explanation: The word 'azzurro' and 'nuvole' (clouds) are Italian.

questions.text: El cielo está azul hoy y no hay nubes.
questions.explanation: The word 'azul' and 'nubes' (clouds) are Spanish.

questions.text: O céu está azul hoje e não há nuvens.
questions.explanation: The word 'céu' and 'nuvens' (clouds) are Portuguese.

questions.text: Ele me disse que viria se tivesse tempo suficiente esta tarde.
questions.explanation: The word 'disse' and 'tivesse' are Portuguese markers.

questions.text: Él me dijo que vendría si tuviera tiempo suficiente esta tarde.
questions.explanation: The word 'dijo' and 'tuviera' identify this as Spanish.

questions.text: Mi ha detto che verrebbe se avesse abbastanza tempo oggi pomeriggio.
questions.explanation: The word 'pomeriggio' and 'abbastanza' are Italian.

questions.text: Il m'a dit qu'il viendrait s'il avait assez de temps cet après-midi.
questions.explanation: The word 'viendrait' and 'après-midi' are French identifiers.

questions.text: Vous devez faire attention en traversant la rue avec les bagages.
questions.explanation: The word 'bagages' and 'rue' (street) are French.

questions.text: Dovete fare attenzione quando attraversate la strada con i bagagli.
questions.explanation: The word 'bagagli' and 'strada' are Italian markers.

questions.text: Debéis tener cuidado al cruzar la calle con el equipaje.
questions.explanation: The word 'calle' and 'equipaje' identify this as Spanish.

questions.text: Devem ter cuidado ao atravessar a rua com as bagagens.
questions.explanation: The word 'atravessar' and 'bagagens' are Portuguese markers.
